title: Memphis Noise Ordinance - Chapter 9.24
text:
Section 9.24.010 - Purpose. The City of Memphis recognizes that excessive noise undermines the quality of life for residents across all neighborhoods. This ordinance establishes maximum permissible sound levels, enforcement procedures, and penalties to protect the health, safety, and welfare of Memphis citizens.

Section 9.24.020 - Definitions. For the purposes of this chapter: (a) 'Ambient noise' means the all-encompassing sound associated with a given environment, excluding the specific noise source under investigation. (b) 'Decibel (dB)' means a unit of measurement of sound pressure level. (c) 'Noise disturbance' means any sound that exceeds the permissible limits set forth in Section 9.24.040 or that endangers the health, safety, or welfare of any person. (d) 'Plainly audible' means any sound that can be detected by a person using unaided hearing faculties from a distance of fifty feet or more from the source.

Section 9.24.040 - Maximum Permissible Sound Levels. Residential zones: 65 dB during daytime hours (7:00 AM to 10:00 PM) and 55 dB during nighttime hours (10:00 PM to 7:00 AM). Commercial zones: 70 dB daytime and 60 dB nighttime. Industrial zones: 80 dB at all times. Entertainment districts along Beale Street and South Main Street may operate at up to 85 dB until 1:00 AM on Friday and Saturday nights under special permit from General Services.

Section 9.24.050 - Exemptions. The following activities are exempt from the noise limits in Section 9.24.040: (a) Emergency vehicles and equipment operated by the Memphis Fire Department or Police; (b) Construction activity between 7:00 AM and 6:00 PM on weekdays with a valid building permit; (c) City-sponsored events at Tom Lee Park, FedExForum, or other designated public venues with approval from the Mayor's Office; (d) Religious institutions during regular services.

Section 9.24.060 - Enforcement. Code Enforcement officers respond to noise complaints filed through the Memphis 311 system. Standard complaints carry a 48-hour response SLA. Emergency noise levels exceeding 95 decibels trigger a 4-hour priority response. First violations receive a written warning. Second violations within 12 months carry a $250 fine. Third and subsequent violations carry fines up to $500 and potential permit revocation for commercial establishments. All fines are payable to the City of Memphis within 30 days or may be contested through the Environmental Court at Shelby County Courthouse.

title: Midtown Zoning Overlay District - Chapter 14.08
text:
Section 14.08.010 - Establishment. The Midtown Zoning Overlay District is hereby established to guide mixed-use development within the area bounded by Poplar Avenue to the north, Central Avenue to the south, Cleveland Street to the west, and East Parkway to the east. This overlay supplements the base zoning classifications with additional standards for building height, setbacks, parking, and design review.

Section 14.08.020 - Purpose. The Midtown Overlay promotes a walkable, transit-friendly urban neighborhood by: (a) encouraging ground-floor commercial uses along Madison Avenue and Cooper Street; (b) permitting residential density up to 40 units per acre on parcels within 500 feet of MATA transit stops; (c) establishing design standards that preserve the architectural character of existing structures near Overton Square and along Young Avenue; (d) requiring minimum 15% green space on new developments exceeding 10,000 square feet.

Section 14.08.030 - Height and Setback Standards. Maximum building height is 55 feet (approximately 4 stories) within 200 feet of single-family residential zones. On parcels fronting Madison Avenue between Cleveland Street and Cooper Street, maximum height is 75 feet with Planning & Development Commission approval. Front setbacks are 0-10 feet on commercial corridors to maintain a continuous streetwall. Side setbacks adjacent to residential parcels require a minimum 15-foot landscaped buffer.

Section 14.08.040 - Parking Requirements. New commercial development requires 1 space per 400 square feet of gross floor area, reduced from the citywide standard of 1 per 300 square feet. Residential development requires 1 space per unit for studios and one-bedrooms, 1.5 spaces per unit for larger units. Developments within 500 feet of a MATA bus stop on Poplar Avenue or Madison Avenue may reduce parking requirements by 25% through a transit proximity waiver approved by Planning & Development.

Section 14.08.050 - Design Review. All new construction and exterior modifications visible from public rights-of-way require design review by the Midtown Design Review Board. Review criteria include: compatibility with adjacent structures, pedestrian-scale design elements, facade articulation on buildings exceeding 60 feet in width, and screened mechanical equipment. Applications are submitted to Planning & Development with a 45-day review period.

title: Downtown Memphis Zoning and Development Standards - Chapter 14.12
text:
Section 14.12.010 - Downtown District Boundaries. The Downtown Memphis Zoning District encompasses the area bounded by the Mississippi River to the west, Danny Thomas Boulevard to the east, I-40 to the north, and E.H. Crump Boulevard to the south. This district includes the Beale Street Entertainment District, the South Main Arts District, the Civic Center area surrounding Memphis City Hall, and the riverfront development zone extending to Mud Island.

Section 14.12.020 - Permitted Uses. The Downtown District permits a broad mix of uses to support a vibrant urban core: (a) Retail and restaurant uses on ground floors of all buildings fronting Beale Street, Main Street, and Front Street; (b) Office and professional services; (c) Hotels and short-term lodging; (d) Residential uses including multifamily, live-work units, and artist studios; (e) Cultural and entertainment venues, including performance halls and museums such as the National Civil Rights Museum; (f) Public and institutional uses including government offices at Memphis City Hall and Shelby County Courthouse.

Section 14.12.030 - Height Standards. No maximum building height within the core downtown area bounded by Front Street, Third Street, Union Avenue, and Beale Street, subject to FAA height restrictions near Memphis International Airport. Buildings within the South Main Arts District are limited to 85 feet to preserve the neighborhood's historic low-rise character. FedExForum and Memphis Convention Center parcels are exempt from general height standards.

Section 14.12.040 - Historic Preservation Requirements. Structures listed on the National Register of Historic Places or designated as Memphis landmarks require Historic Preservation Commission review before any exterior alteration, demolition, or new construction within 100 feet. The Beale Street Historic District and South Main Historic District have additional facade preservation guidelines administered jointly by Planning & Development and the Memphis Heritage Foundation.

Section 14.12.050 - Incentives. The Downtown Memphis Community Redevelopment Agency offers tax increment financing (TIF) for qualifying projects that create at least 20 housing units or 10,000 square feet of commercial space. Payment in lieu of taxes (PILOT) programs are available for developments exceeding $5 million in investment. Applications are reviewed quarterly by the Housing & Community Development department.

title: East Memphis Residential Zoning Standards - Chapter 14.16
text:
Section 14.16.010 - Purpose. The East Memphis Residential Zoning Standards preserve the suburban residential character of neighborhoods along Walnut Grove Road, Perkins Road, and White Station Road while allowing compatible commercial development on designated corridors. These standards reflect the community's commitment to maintaining low-density residential neighborhoods, quality schools, and neighborhood parks including Shelby Farms Park.

Section 14.16.020 - Residential Zone Classifications. R-1 Single Family: Minimum lot size 8,000 square feet, maximum building coverage 35%, maximum height 35 feet (2.5 stories). R-2 Single Family: Minimum lot size 6,000 square feet, maximum building coverage 40%, maximum height 35 feet. R-3 Multifamily: Permitted only on parcels fronting Poplar Avenue, Walnut Grove Road, or Mendenhall Road, maximum density 20 units per acre, maximum height 45 feet with Planning & Development approval.

Section 14.16.030 - Commercial Corridor Standards. Commercial uses are permitted along Poplar Avenue between Perkins Road and Germantown Parkway, and along Walnut Grove Road between Highland Street and White Station Road. The Oak Court Mall site and Carrefour at Kirby are designated as Planned Commercial Districts with individual site plans approved by Planning & Development. New drive-through restaurants require a conditional use permit and must demonstrate minimal traffic impact on adjacent residential streets.

Section 14.16.040 - Tree Preservation. All residential lots in East Memphis are subject to the city's Tree Protection Ordinance (Chapter 8.16). Removal of any tree with a trunk diameter exceeding 12 inches at breast height requires a permit from Parks & Recreation. Developers must preserve at least 40% of existing mature trees on any parcel undergoing subdivision or redevelopment. Replacement trees must be native species approved by the Memphis Urban Forester and planted within 6 months of removal.

Section 14.16.050 - Stormwater Management. Due to flooding concerns along Nonconnah Creek and Wolf River tributaries, all new construction in East Memphis must comply with enhanced stormwater detention standards. Impervious surface coverage on residential parcels may not exceed 50%. Commercial developments must provide on-site detention capacity for the 25-year storm event. Green infrastructure techniques including rain gardens, permeable pavers, and bioswales are encouraged and qualify for a 10% reduction in stormwater fees.

title: Memphis Building Code Standards - Chapter 12.04
text:
Section 12.04.010 - Adoption. The City of Memphis adopts the 2024 International Building Code (IBC) with local amendments as specified in this chapter. All new construction, renovations, and additions within the city limits must comply with these standards. The Building Official within Planning & Development is authorized to enforce these provisions and issue building permits.

Section 12.04.020 - Permit Requirements. A building permit is required before commencing any of the following: (a) New construction of any structure; (b) Structural alterations or additions to existing buildings; (c) Demolition of any structure; (d) Installation or modification of electrical, plumbing, or mechanical systems; (e) Change of occupancy classification. Permit applications must include architectural drawings, structural calculations where applicable, and proof of property ownership or authorized agent status. The permit fee schedule is set by General Services and reviewed annually by City Council.

Section 12.04.030 - Inspection Requirements. All permitted work is subject to inspection by Code Enforcement at the following stages: (a) Foundation, before concrete placement; (b) Framing, before wall covering; (c) Rough-in for electrical, plumbing, and mechanical systems; (d) Insulation and energy compliance; (e) Final inspection before certificate of occupancy. Inspections must be requested at least 48 hours in advance through the Memphis 311 system or online portal. Failed inspections require correction and re-inspection within 30 days.

Section 12.04.040 - Residential Standards. Single-family and duplex structures must meet the 2024 International Residential Code (IRC) as adopted by Tennessee. Minimum habitable room area is 70 square feet with a minimum dimension of 7 feet. Ceiling heights must be at least 7 feet 6 inches in habitable rooms. All sleeping rooms must have at least one egress window meeting IRC Section R310 requirements. Smoke detectors are required on each level and in every sleeping room, with carbon monoxide detectors required where fuel-burning appliances are present.

Section 12.04.050 - Enforcement and Penalties. Work performed without a required permit is subject to double permit fees upon discovery. Stop-work orders may be issued by Code Enforcement for any construction activity that violates these standards or poses an immediate safety hazard. Violations of the building code are Class C misdemeanors punishable by fines up to $500 per day of violation. The Fire Department has concurrent jurisdiction over fire safety provisions in commercial and multifamily structures.

title: Short-Term Rental Regulations - Chapter 6.22
text:
Section 6.22.010 - Purpose. This chapter establishes regulations for short-term rental properties in the Cooper-Young neighborhood and surrounding residential areas to balance the economic benefits of tourism with the preservation of neighborhood character. Short-term rentals have increased significantly along Young Avenue, Cooper Street, and in residential areas near Overton Park and Overton Square, prompting the need for a regulatory framework.

Section 6.22.020 - Definitions. (a) 'Short-term rental' means a residential dwelling unit, or portion thereof, offered for temporary lodging for a period of less than 30 consecutive days in exchange for compensation. (b) 'Owner-occupied rental' means a short-term rental where the property owner maintains the unit as their primary residence and is present during guest stays. (c) 'Non-owner-occupied rental' means a short-term rental where the property owner does not reside at the property during guest stays.

Section 6.22.030 - Permit Requirements. All short-term rental operators must obtain a Short-Term Rental Permit from General Services before listing any property on hosting platforms. Permits are valid for one year from date of issuance and must be renewed annually. Application requirements include: (a) Proof of property ownership; (b) Floor plan showing sleeping areas, exits, and parking; (c) Proof of homeowner's insurance covering short-term rental activity; (d) Written acknowledgment of noise ordinance compliance; (e) Designated local contact person available 24/7 within a 30-minute response radius.

Section 6.22.040 - Operating Standards. Maximum occupancy is 2 guests per designated sleeping room plus 2 additional guests, not to exceed 10 guests total. Quiet hours are 10:00 PM to 7:00 AM per the citywide Noise Ordinance (Chapter 9.24). Off-street parking must be provided at a rate of 1 space per 2 guests. Exterior signage advertising the rental is prohibited in residential zones. Trash must be placed in city-provided containers and brought to the curb only on scheduled collection days per Solid Waste Management guidelines.

Section 6.22.050 - Enforcement. Code Enforcement officers respond to complaints about short-term rentals filed through the Memphis 311 system. First violations receive a written warning. Second violations within 12 months carry a $500 fine and mandatory 90-day permit suspension. Third violations result in permit revocation with a 2-year prohibition on reapplication. The city reserves the right to revoke permits for properties generating more than 3 verified noise complaints within any 6-month period.

title: Memphis Sign Regulations - Chapter 10.12
text:
Section 10.12.010 - Purpose. This chapter regulates the size, placement, illumination, and maintenance of signs within the City of Memphis to protect public safety, preserve aesthetic quality, and ensure that signs effectively serve their communicative purpose without creating visual clutter or traffic hazards.

Section 10.12.020 - Permit Requirements. A sign permit from Planning & Development is required for all permanent signs, including wall signs, freestanding signs, monument signs, projecting signs, and electronic message centers. Temporary signs for special events, real estate, and construction are permitted without a permit for up to 90 days. Political signs are exempt from permitting but must be removed within 7 days after an election.

Section 10.12.030 - Size and Height Standards by Zone. In residential zones (R-1 through R-3): Maximum sign area of 6 square feet for home occupation signs, maximum height of 4 feet for freestanding signs. In commercial zones: Wall signs may not exceed 15% of the building facade area or 200 square feet, whichever is less. Freestanding signs are limited to 32 square feet in area and 20 feet in height. Along Poplar Avenue, Walnut Grove Road, and Germantown Parkway, freestanding signs may be up to 25 feet in height with a monument-style base.

Section 10.12.040 - Electronic Message Centers. Electronic signs (LED, digital) are permitted in commercial and industrial zones only. Minimum display time per message is 8 seconds. Transition effects must be instantaneous (no scrolling, flashing, or animation). Brightness must not exceed 0.3 foot-candles above ambient light levels measured at the property line. Electronic signs within 300 feet of a residential zone must dim to 50% intensity between 10:00 PM and 6:00 AM. All electronic signs require an automatic dimming sensor.

Section 10.12.050 - Prohibited Signs. The following signs are prohibited throughout the City of Memphis: (a) Off-premises advertising signs (billboards) in residential zones; (b) Signs attached to utility poles, traffic signs, or trees on public property; (c) Roof-mounted signs that extend above the roofline; (d) Signs that obstruct traffic visibility at intersections; (e) Abandoned signs (not maintained for 12 months) which must be removed by the property owner within 30 days of notice from Code Enforcement.

title: Memphis Animal Control Ordinance - Chapter 5.08
text:
Section 5.08.010 - Purpose. This chapter establishes regulations for the ownership, care, and control of animals within the City of Memphis to protect public health and safety, prevent animal cruelty, and manage the city's animal population through responsible ownership standards and enforcement.

Section 5.08.020 - Licensing. All dogs and cats over the age of 4 months must be licensed annually with General Services. License fees are $15 for spayed/neutered animals and $50 for unaltered animals. Proof of current rabies vaccination is required at the time of licensing. License tags must be worn on a collar or harness at all times when the animal is off the owner's property. Failure to license carries a $75 fine per animal.

Section 5.08.030 - Leash and Confinement Requirements. Dogs must be on a leash not exceeding 8 feet in length when on any public property, sidewalk, or street. Dogs must be confined to the owner's property by a fence, enclosure, or electronic containment system at all other times. Exceptions apply at designated off-leash areas in Overton Park (east meadow area) and Shelby Farms Park (designated dog park). Cats are exempt from leash requirements but may not be allowed to roam on neighboring properties if complaints are filed.

Section 5.08.040 - Dangerous Animal Provisions. An animal may be declared dangerous by Code Enforcement after: (a) An unprovoked bite causing injury to a person; (b) Killing or injuring a domestic animal while off the owner's property; (c) Two or more incidents of threatening behavior documented by witnesses. Owners of declared dangerous animals must maintain $100,000 liability insurance, confine the animal in a secure enclosure with posted warning signs, and muzzle the animal when in public. Dangerous animal registrations must be renewed semi-annually.

Section 5.08.050 - Stray Animal Management. Memphis Animal Services operates the city shelter on Tchulahoma Road, providing intake services for stray, surrendered, and seized animals. Stray animals are held for a 5-day redemption period during which owners may reclaim them by paying impound fees ($50 first offense, $100 second offense, $200 third and subsequent). Unclaimed animals are evaluated for adoption suitability. The city partners with local rescue organizations including Memphis Humane Society for placement of adoptable animals. Citizens may report stray animals through the Memphis 311 system with an expected response time of 24 hours from General Services.

title: Memphis Sidewalk Maintenance and Repair Standards - Chapter 7.16
text:
Section 7.16.010 - Purpose. This chapter establishes standards for the construction, maintenance, and repair of sidewalks within the City of Memphis to ensure safe pedestrian passage, comply with the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), and support the city's Complete Streets policy adopted by City Council in 2023.

Section 7.16.020 - Responsibility for Maintenance. Property owners are responsible for maintaining sidewalks adjacent to their property in a safe, passable condition. This includes: (a) Repair of cracks, heaving, or settling that creates a vertical displacement exceeding 0.5 inches; (b) Removal of vegetation encroaching onto the sidewalk surface; (c) Snow and ice removal within 24 hours of cessation of precipitation; (d) Keeping sidewalks clear of obstructions including trash containers, except during designated collection hours per Solid Waste Management schedules.

Section 7.16.030 - Construction Standards. New sidewalks must be constructed of Portland cement concrete at least 4 inches thick on a compacted aggregate base. Minimum width is 5 feet in residential zones and 8 feet in commercial zones and along designated corridors including Poplar Avenue, Madison Avenue, Union Avenue, and Main Street. Curb ramps compliant with ADA standards (maximum 8.33% slope, detectable warning surface) are required at all intersections and mid-block crossings. Expansion joints must be placed at 5-foot intervals.

Section 7.16.040 - Repair and Replacement Program. Public Works maintains a priority system for sidewalk repair: Priority 1 — Vertical displacement exceeding 2 inches or complete panel failure (repair within 30 days). Priority 2 — Vertical displacement between 1 and 2 inches (repair within 90 days). Priority 3 — Cosmetic damage or displacement under 1 inch (scheduled during annual maintenance). Citizens may report sidewalk damage through the Memphis 311 system. Public Works - Streets division responds with assessment within 10 business days.

Section 7.16.050 - Cost Sharing. The city provides a 50/50 cost-sharing program for sidewalk replacement on residential properties. The maximum city contribution is $2,500 per property per fiscal year. Applications are submitted to Public Works and funded on a first-come, first-served basis from the annual sidewalk repair budget. Properties within the Midtown, Downtown, and Cooper-Young neighborhoods receive priority funding due to higher pedestrian traffic volumes. Commercial property owners are responsible for 100% of sidewalk repair costs adjacent to their parcels.

title: Memphis Tree Protection Ordinance - Chapter 8.16
text:
Section 8.16.010 - Purpose. The City of Memphis recognizes the significant environmental, aesthetic, and economic value of its urban tree canopy. This ordinance protects mature trees on public and private property, establishes a permitting process for tree removal, and requires replacement planting to maintain the city's goal of 40% urban canopy coverage by 2030.

Section 8.16.020 - Protected Trees. A 'protected tree' is any tree with a trunk diameter at breast height (DBH, measured at 4.5 feet above grade) of 12 inches or greater on private property, or any tree on public property regardless of size. Species of special concern include: Southern magnolia, live oak, bald cypress, American elm, and pecan trees, which are subject to additional protections due to their ecological significance and slow growth rates.

Section 8.16.030 - Removal Permits. No person shall remove, destroy, or cause irreparable damage to a protected tree without first obtaining a Tree Removal Permit from Parks & Recreation. Applications must include: (a) Species identification and DBH measurement; (b) Reason for removal (dead, diseased, hazardous, construction necessity); (c) Site plan showing all trees on the parcel; (d) Proposed replacement planting plan. Permits for removal of healthy trees for construction purposes require replacement at a 2:1 ratio (two replacement trees for each tree removed). Replacement trees must be at least 2-inch caliper at planting.

Section 8.16.040 - Tree Preservation During Construction. Any construction project requiring a building permit must submit a Tree Protection Plan if the site contains protected trees. Protection measures include: (a) Protective fencing at the drip line of all trees to be preserved; (b) No equipment storage, material stockpiling, or grade changes within the tree protection zone; (c) Root pruning by a certified arborist if excavation must occur within the drip line; (d) Regular monitoring by the project arborist with reports to Parks & Recreation.

Section 8.16.050 - Penalties. Unauthorized removal of a protected tree carries penalties based on the tree's appraised value using the International Society of Arboriculture (ISA) methodology: trees valued under $5,000 carry a fine equal to the appraised value; trees valued over $5,000 carry a fine of 1.5 times the appraised value. Additionally, violators must plant replacement trees per the 2:1 ratio within 6 months. Failure to plant replacements incurs an additional fine of $500 per tree. The Memphis Urban Forester within Parks & Recreation is authorized to conduct enforcement inspections.

title: Memphis Historic Preservation Ordinance - Chapter 4.20
text:
Section 4.20.010 - Purpose. The City of Memphis establishes this Historic Preservation Ordinance to identify, protect, and promote the preservation of buildings, structures, sites, and districts of historical, architectural, and cultural significance. Memphis's rich heritage, from the Beale Street Historic District to the Victorian neighborhoods of Central Gardens, represents an irreplaceable resource that contributes to the city's identity and economic vitality.

Section 4.20.020 - Historic Preservation Commission. The Memphis Historic Preservation Commission (HPC) consists of nine members appointed by the Mayor and confirmed by City Council. Members must include at least one licensed architect, one historian, and one real estate professional. The HPC reviews applications for exterior alterations, demolitions, and new construction within designated historic districts and for individually designated landmarks.

Section 4.20.030 - Designated Historic Districts. The following areas are designated as Memphis Historic Districts: (a) Beale Street Historic District (Downtown) — nationally significant as the birthplace of the blues; (b) South Main Historic District — early 20th-century commercial and warehouse architecture along South Main Street; (c) Victorian Village (Downtown) — cluster of antebellum and Victorian-era mansions along Adams Avenue; (d) Central Gardens (Midtown) — early 20th-century residential neighborhood between Peabody Avenue and Central Avenue; (e) Cooper-Young Historic Overlay — bungalow and craftsman-style residential streets surrounding the Cooper-Young intersection.

Section 4.20.040 - Certificate of Appropriateness. No person shall undertake any exterior alteration, addition, demolition, or new construction within a historic district or affecting a designated landmark without first obtaining a Certificate of Appropriateness (COA) from the HPC. Applications must demonstrate that proposed work is compatible with the historic character of the district. The HPC uses the Secretary of the Interior's Standards for the Treatment of Historic Properties as the basis for review.

Section 4.20.050 - Demolition Review. Demolition of any structure within a historic district or designated as a landmark requires a 90-day delay period during which the HPC and Housing & Community Development seek alternatives to demolition, including adaptive reuse, sale to a preservation buyer, or relocation. Demolition by neglect — allowing a historic structure to deteriorate through willful neglect of maintenance — is a violation carrying fines of $100 per day and mandatory stabilization by the property owner.

title: Memphis Food Truck Permit Regulations - Chapter 6.30
text:
Section 6.30.010 - Purpose. This chapter establishes a permitting and regulatory framework for mobile food vending units (food trucks) operating within the City of Memphis. The food truck industry has grown significantly, particularly in the Downtown, Midtown, and Cooper-Young areas, providing diverse dining options and supporting small business entrepreneurship.

Section 6.30.020 - Permit Requirements. All food truck operators must obtain a Mobile Food Vendor Permit from General Services before commencing operations. Permits are valid for one calendar year and cost $500 annually. Application requirements include: (a) Valid Tennessee Department of Health food service establishment permit; (b) Shelby County Health Department inspection certificate; (c) Memphis business license; (d) Proof of liability insurance ($1,000,000 minimum); (e) Vehicle registration and annual safety inspection; (f) Commissary agreement with a licensed commercial kitchen for food preparation and vehicle cleaning.

Section 6.30.030 - Operating Locations and Hours. Food trucks may operate on private property with written permission of the property owner. On public property, food trucks are permitted in designated Mobile Food Vending Zones established by Planning & Development: (a) Downtown — Main Street Mall, Court Square, and the riverfront promenade near Tom Lee Park (maximum 6 trucks per zone); (b) Midtown — Overton Square parking areas and the Crosstown Concourse plaza (maximum 4 trucks); (c) Medical District — designated lots on Union Avenue near Methodist Hospital. Operating hours are 7:00 AM to 11:00 PM daily. Food trucks must maintain a minimum distance of 200 feet from the entrance of any brick-and-mortar restaurant with a dine-in license.

Section 6.30.040 - Health and Safety Standards. All food trucks must comply with the Shelby County Food Code and maintain: (a) Potable water supply of at least 40 gallons; (b) Wastewater containment of at least 50 gallons; (c) Adequate refrigeration for perishable items at or below 41 degrees Fahrenheit; (d) Fire suppression system for cooking equipment; (e) Visible posted permit and most recent health inspection score. Waste disposal must use the nearest public trash receptacles or be carried out by the operator. No wastewater discharge is permitted on streets or into storm drains.

Section 6.30.050 - Enforcement. Code Enforcement and the Shelby County Health Department jointly enforce food truck regulations. First violations receive a written warning. Second violations within 12 months carry a $250 fine. Third violations result in a 30-day permit suspension. Operation without a permit carries a $500 fine and immediate impoundment of the vehicle. Complaints may be filed through the Memphis 311 system.

title: Midtown Memphis Master Development Plan 2025-2030
text:
Executive Summary. The Midtown Memphis Master Development Plan establishes a comprehensive framework for sustainable growth in the Midtown district over the next five years. Midtown, bounded by Poplar Avenue to the north, Central Avenue to the south, Cleveland Street to the west, and East Parkway to the east, serves as the geographic and cultural heart of Memphis. This plan addresses housing affordability, commercial corridor revitalization, transportation improvements, and green space preservation.

Housing Strategy. The plan targets 1,200 new housing units by 2030, with a minimum 20% affordable housing set-aside. Priority development zones include the Overton Square corridor along Madison Avenue, the Cooper Street infill area, and underutilized parcels along Sam Cooper Boulevard. Mixed-use zoning overlay districts will encourage ground-floor commercial with upper-floor residential development, following the successful Crosstown Concourse model. Housing & Community Development will administer a $3 million annual incentive fund for developers meeting the affordability threshold.

Transportation Improvements. The plan allocates $15 million for pedestrian and cycling infrastructure, including protected bike lanes on Madison Avenue and Poplar Avenue, new sidewalks along residential streets, and enhanced MATA bus shelters at 12 high-ridership stops. A trolley extension from South Main to Overton Park is under feasibility study with an estimated $45 million capital cost. Public Works will implement traffic calming measures on residential streets including speed tables, curb extensions, and intersection murals.

Green Space and Sustainability. Overton Park will receive $3.2 million in trail improvements and native plantings. The plan establishes a tree canopy target of 40% coverage for residential streets by 2030, administered by Parks & Recreation. Stormwater management investments include bioswales along Peabody Avenue, rain gardens at three Midtown schools, and a pilot green alley program converting traditional concrete alleys to permeable surfaces. The plan supports Memphis's Climate Action Plan goal of reducing municipal carbon emissions by 30% by 2030.

title: Downtown Memphis Revitalization Strategy 2025-2028
text:
Vision Statement. Downtown Memphis will be the premier urban destination in the Mid-South, combining world-class entertainment along Beale Street with thriving residential neighborhoods, a robust business district, and an accessible riverfront that reconnects the city to the Mississippi River. This three-year strategy coordinates public and private investment to achieve measurable growth in residential population, commercial occupancy, and visitor traffic.

Residential Growth. Downtown's residential population has grown from 2,800 in 2015 to approximately 5,200 in 2024. This plan targets 8,000 residents by 2028 through: (a) Adaptive reuse of 6 vacant warehouse buildings along Front Street and South Main Street into 400 residential units; (b) New construction of 350 units on the former Convention Center auxiliary parking site; (c) Live-work artist studios in the South Main Arts District; (d) Micro-unit development targeting young professionals near FedExForum. Housing & Community Development will offer PILOT incentives for projects that include at least 15% workforce housing units.

Riverfront Development. The $65 million Tom Lee Park redesign, completed in 2024, created 30 acres of accessible riverfront green space. Phase 2 investments include: (a) Pedestrian bridge connecting the bluffs to Mud Island; (b) Kayak and paddleboard launch facilities; (c) Year-round event pavilion for concerts and festivals; (d) Extended Riverwalk trail connecting Tom Lee Park to Martyrs Park. The Mayor's Office will seek federal Transportation Alternatives Program funding for the pedestrian bridge.

Public Safety and Infrastructure. The plan allocates $8 million for Downtown infrastructure improvements: upgraded street lighting on Main Street and Union Avenue using energy-efficient LED fixtures, expanded security camera network (75 new cameras integrated with the Memphis Real Time Crime Center), rehabilitation of the historic cobblestone streets on Front Street, and modernized traffic signals at 15 intersections along Danny Thomas Boulevard and Third Street. Police Community Relations will expand the Downtown foot patrol program from 8 to 16 officers.

Economic Development Targets. The strategy targets creation of 2,500 new jobs downtown by 2028, with emphasis on technology, creative industries, and hospitality sectors. The Downtown Memphis Commission will manage a $2 million annual small business facade improvement grant program for storefronts along Main Street and Beale Street.

title: Whitehaven Economic Development Action Plan
text:
Introduction. Whitehaven, located in south Memphis along Elvis Presley Boulevard, represents one of Memphis's most historically significant neighborhoods. Home to Graceland, the second most-visited private residence in America, Whitehaven attracts over 600,000 tourists annually. However, the surrounding commercial corridors have experienced disinvestment. This action plan leverages tourism assets and community partnerships to stimulate economic revitalization.

Commercial Corridor Strategy. Elvis Presley Boulevard from Brooks Road to Shelby Drive will be designated a Priority Economic Development Zone. Investments include: (a) Streetscape improvements — new sidewalks, decorative lighting, and wayfinding signage connecting Graceland to local businesses; (b) Facade improvement grants of up to $25,000 per business for exterior renovations; (c) Targeted business recruitment focusing on dining, retail, and cultural attractions that complement the Graceland tourism market; (d) Establishment of a Whitehaven Business Improvement District (BID) with annual assessments funding maintenance, marketing, and security.

Workforce Development. The plan partners with Southwest Tennessee Community College and Memphis Goodwill to create a Whitehaven Workforce Center offering: (a) Hospitality industry certification programs serving the Graceland tourism economy; (b) Small business entrepreneurship workshops; (c) Digital literacy and job readiness training; (d) Youth employment pipeline connecting Whitehaven high school students with local employers. The Mayor's Office Youth Employment Program will reserve 50 summer positions for Whitehaven residents.

Housing Stabilization. Whitehaven's housing stock includes many mid-century homes requiring maintenance investment. The plan establishes: (a) A home repair grant program for owner-occupied properties (income-qualified, maximum $15,000 per home) administered by Housing & Community Development; (b) Code Enforcement task force targeting blighted and abandoned properties; (c) Land bank acquisition of tax-delinquent parcels for community land trust development; (d) Partnership with Habitat for Humanity Memphis for new affordable homeownership units.

Transportation Access. Whitehaven currently has limited public transit frequency. The plan advocates for: (a) Increased MATA Route 34 (Elvis Presley) service to 15-minute headways during peak hours; (b) New bus shelter installations at 8 high-ridership stops; (c) Pedestrian safety improvements at the Elvis Presley Boulevard and Brooks Road intersection, including a HAWK pedestrian signal and median refuge island.

title: Frayser Neighborhood Revitalization Plan
text:
Community Vision. The Frayser neighborhood, located in north Memphis along Stage Road and Raleigh-Millington Road, envisions a safe, connected community with quality housing, accessible services, and economic opportunity for all residents. This plan was developed through 18 months of community engagement including 12 public meetings, 3 neighborhood walks, and surveys of over 800 Frayser residents.

Safety and Blight Reduction. Frayser's primary challenge is the concentration of vacant and abandoned properties, which contribute to public safety concerns and depress property values. The plan calls for: (a) Demolition of 150 severely blighted structures over 3 years using HUD CDBG funds; (b) Side-lot disposition program allowing adjacent homeowners to purchase vacant lots for $100 each; (c) Community clean-up events quarterly, coordinated by Solid Waste Management with volunteer support; (d) Installation of 25 solar-powered security lights on residential streets identified through Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design (CPTED) analysis.

Housing Investment. The plan targets rehabilitation of 200 existing homes and construction of 75 new affordable homes by 2028. Funding sources include: (a) Tennessee Housing Development Agency (THDA) HOME funds; (b) Federal Home Loan Bank Affordable Housing Program grants; (c) City of Memphis Housing Trust Fund; (d) Private developer partnerships leveraging Low-Income Housing Tax Credits (LIHTC). Housing & Community Development will serve as lead implementing agency. All new construction must meet Enterprise Green Communities standards for sustainable, healthy housing.

Youth and Education. The Frayser Community Schools Initiative partners Memphis public schools with community organizations to provide wraparound services: after-school tutoring, mental health counseling, family resource coordination, and summer enrichment programs. The plan allocates $500,000 annually from the city's Community Development Block Grant for youth programming at the Frayser Community Center.

Economic Opportunity. A Frayser Main Street program will be established to revitalize the Thomas Street commercial corridor, including: (a) Small business microloans of $5,000-$25,000 through CDFI partnership; (b) Commercial kitchen incubator for food entrepreneurs; (c) Farmers market at the Frayser Gateway site, open Saturdays April through October.

title: Orange Mound Historic Preservation and Cultural Heritage Plan
text:
Historical Significance. Orange Mound holds a unique place in American history as one of the first neighborhoods in the United States built by and for African Americans. Established in the 1890s on the former Deaderick plantation along Lamar Avenue and Airways Boulevard, Orange Mound has been a center of African American cultural life in Memphis for over 125 years. This preservation plan recognizes that history and proposes strategies to honor it while addressing contemporary community needs.

National Register Nomination. The plan initiates the process to nominate the Orange Mound Historic District to the National Register of Historic Places. The proposed district encompasses approximately 800 acres bounded by Park Avenue, Airways Boulevard, Lamar Avenue, and the Norfolk Southern railroad corridor. Key contributing structures include the Orange Mound Community Center, Melrose High School, and approximately 200 homes dating from 1900 to 1950 representing vernacular shotgun, bungalow, and minimal traditional architectural styles.

Heritage Tourism. The plan proposes a self-guided Orange Mound Heritage Trail featuring: (a) 15 interpretive markers at significant historical sites; (b) A cultural center and museum in the renovated Orange Mound Community Center, managed by Parks & Recreation; (c) Partnership with the Memphis Music Initiative to document Orange Mound's contributions to blues, soul, and gospel music; (d) Annual Orange Mound Heritage Festival with city support through the Mayor's Office of Cultural Affairs.

Housing Preservation. Many of Orange Mound's historic homes require significant rehabilitation. The plan establishes: (a) Historic rehabilitation tax credits for contributing structures within the proposed National Register district; (b) Emergency stabilization fund for homes at risk of demolition by neglect — $500,000 administered by Housing & Community Development; (c) Partnership with the University of Memphis School of Architecture for a Community Design Center providing pro-bono renovation design services; (d) Lead paint and asbestos abatement program for pre-1950 homes, funded through EPA brownfield grants.

Community Infrastructure. Investment priorities include: (a) Reconstruction of sidewalks on Carnes Avenue and Deaderick Avenue (Public Works); (b) Renovation of the Orange Mound Park swimming pool and playground; (c) Improved street lighting on Airways Boulevard and Park Avenue; (d) Community garden program on 5 vacant city-owned parcels.

title: Binghampton Arts District Strategic Plan
text:
District Vision. The Binghampton Arts District will be Memphis's premier live-work-create neighborhood, where affordable studio space, community galleries, and maker workshops coexist with quality housing and neighborhood-serving retail along Broad Avenue and Tillman Street. Building on the success of the Broad Avenue Arts District commercial corridor, this plan expands the creative economy across the broader Binghampton neighborhood.

Arts Infrastructure. The plan proposes: (a) Conversion of the former Memphis Cotton Exchange warehouse on Evelyn Avenue into a 20,000-square-foot arts incubator with 15 subsidized studio spaces, communal workshop areas, and a public gallery managed by a nonprofit arts organization; (b) Public art installations at 8 locations along Broad Avenue and Sam Cooper Boulevard, funded through the city's Percent for Art program; (c) Outdoor performance space at the Binghampton Community Garden site for concerts, theater, and film screenings; (d) Artist-in-residence program providing 6-month studio and housing stipends for 4 artists annually.

Housing Strategy. Binghampton's revitalization must balance new investment with anti-displacement protections for long-term residents. The plan requires: (a) All new residential developments receiving city incentives to include 25% of units at or below 60% Area Median Income (AMI); (b) Community Land Trust (CLT) acquisition of 30 parcels for permanently affordable homeownership; (c) Tenant protection ordinance requiring 12-month notice before any rent increase exceeding 10% in the Arts District overlay area; (d) Home repair assistance for existing homeowners through Housing & Community Development, prioritizing seniors and families below 80% AMI.

Commercial Development. The Broad Avenue corridor has attracted independent restaurants, galleries, and retail shops. The plan supports continued growth through: (a) Commercial rent stabilization in city-owned properties leased to small businesses; (b) Small business technical assistance and marketing support through the Binghampton Development Corporation; (c) Food and beverage cluster development around the Broad Avenue and Tillman Street intersection; (d) Improved parking management including a shared lot agreement with area churches.

Transportation. Binghampton benefits from proximity to Sam Cooper Boulevard and the Shelby Farms Greenline. The plan adds: (a) Protected bike lane on Broad Avenue connecting to the Greenline trail; (b) Enhanced MATA bus stop amenities on Sam Cooper Boulevard; (c) Pedestrian improvements at the Broad Avenue and Hollywood Street intersection.

title: Crosstown Mixed-Use Expansion Framework
text:
Context. The adaptive reuse of the former Sears Crosstown building into Crosstown Concourse in 2017 transformed the Crosstown neighborhood and established a national model for mixed-use redevelopment. This framework plan guides complementary development in the surrounding blocks to build on that momentum while preserving neighborhood affordability and character.

Development Opportunity Sites. The framework identifies 12 underutilized parcels within a half-mile radius of Crosstown Concourse totaling 18 acres of development potential. Priority sites include: (a) The former industrial parcels along Watkins Street (4.5 acres) — recommended for mixed-income housing with ground-floor neighborhood retail; (b) Vacant land at Cleveland Street and North Parkway (2.8 acres) — recommended for a community health center and workforce housing; (c) The Sears parking structure site (1.2 acres) — recommended for structured parking with active ground-floor uses.

Density and Design Standards. The framework establishes: (a) Maximum building height of 65 feet within the Crosstown Mixed-Use overlay; (b) Required ground-floor activation on all parcels fronting Watkins Street and North Parkway; (c) Building facade design must complement the industrial character of Crosstown Concourse using materials such as brick, steel, and large-format windows; (d) Minimum 10% public open space on developments exceeding one acre.

Infrastructure Investment. The framework recommends $12 million in public infrastructure improvements: (a) Reconstruction of Watkins Street with bike lanes, widened sidewalks, and street trees by Public Works; (b) New MATA transit hub at Cleveland Street and North Parkway with real-time arrival information; (c) Stormwater detention improvements addressing chronic flooding at the Cleveland Street underpass; (d) Underground utility relocation on North Parkway to improve the pedestrian environment.

Community Benefits Framework. All developments receiving public incentives (TIF, PILOT, or density bonuses) must provide community benefits including: living wage employment for construction and permanent jobs, local hiring preference with a goal of 30% Memphis residents, affordable housing set-asides, and contribution to a neighborhood improvement fund managed by the Crosstown Community Development Corporation.

title: South Memphis Workforce Development Corridor Plan
text:
Overview. The South Memphis Workforce Development Corridor Plan targets the area along Elvis Presley Boulevard from E.H. Crump Boulevard south to Brooks Road, connecting the Downtown employment center to the Whitehaven tourism economy. This corridor passes through the historically significant Soulsville neighborhood, home to the Stax Museum of American Soul Music, and represents one of Memphis's greatest opportunities for inclusive economic development.

Workforce Hub. The plan proposes a South Memphis Workforce Hub at the intersection of Elvis Presley Boulevard and McLemore Avenue, adjacent to the Stax Music Academy. The hub will house: (a) Job training programs in healthcare, logistics, and manufacturing — sectors with strong Memphis employment demand; (b) Adult education and GED programs; (c) Digital skills lab with 30 computer workstations and free high-speed internet; (d) Career counseling and job placement services. The hub will be developed through a partnership between the Mayor's Office, Southwest Tennessee Community College, and the Greater Memphis Chamber of Commerce.

CorridorInfrastructure. Public Works investments include: (a) Complete reconstruction of Elvis Presley Boulevard between Crump and Brooks Road with new curb, gutter, and sidewalks; (b) Improved street lighting using crime-deterrent LED fixtures; (c) New MATA bus shelters at 6 stops along the corridor; (d) Gateway monument at the Elvis Presley Boulevard and Crump Boulevard intersection marking the entrance to the Soulsville cultural district.

Small Business Support. The plan creates a South Memphis Small Business Accelerator offering: (a) Microloans of $2,500-$15,000 at below-market rates; (b) Shared commercial kitchen for food entrepreneurs; (c) Pop-up retail spaces for testing business concepts; (d) Mentorship matching with established Memphis business owners. General Services will streamline permitting for new businesses within the corridor, targeting permit issuance within 15 business days.

Community Health. The plan addresses health disparities in South Memphis through: (a) Recruitment of a federally qualified health center (FQHC) to the corridor; (b) Fresh food access through a weekly farmers market and establishment of a neighborhood grocery cooperative; (c) Safe walking routes connecting residential areas to schools and the Stax Museum campus; (d) Parks & Recreation programming at Martin Luther King Jr. Park including fitness classes, walking clubs, and youth sports leagues.

title: Raleigh Community Development Strategy
text:
Community Profile. Raleigh, located in north Memphis along Stage Road and Raleigh-Millington Road, is a community of approximately 35,000 residents characterized by post-war suburban development, commercial corridors anchored by the former Raleigh Springs Mall site, and strong community institutions. This development strategy addresses commercial vacancy, infrastructure aging, and the need for quality community services.

Raleigh Springs Town Center. The redevelopment of the former Raleigh Springs Mall site is the centerpiece of this strategy. The 85-acre site will be transformed into a mixed-use town center including: (a) 250 residential units in a mix of townhomes and garden apartments; (b) 80,000 square feet of neighborhood retail anchored by a grocery store; (c) A 15,000-square-foot community center and branch library operated by Parks & Recreation and the Memphis Public Library system; (d) 5-acre public park with playground, walking trails, and community event space; (e) 25,000 square feet of office space targeting healthcare providers and professional services.

Commercial Corridor Improvements. Stage Road and Austin Peay Highway will receive streetscape investments including: (a) Median landscaping and wayfinding signage from Raleigh-Millington Road to Austin Peay; (b) Access management improvements to reduce traffic conflicts at commercial driveways; (c) Facade improvement grants for businesses along Stage Road (matching grants up to $15,000 administered by Planning & Development); (d) Code Enforcement blitz targeting abandoned commercial properties for compliance or demolition.

Housing Quality. The plan addresses housing stock maintenance through: (a) Single-family rehabilitation program targeting 100 homes over 5 years; (b) Rental registration and inspection program ensuring minimum habitability standards in multifamily properties; (c) Infill housing on vacant lots following neighborhood-compatible design standards; (d) Senior housing development on a 3-acre parcel at Gaisman Park. Housing & Community Development will administer all housing programs.

Infrastructure. Priority infrastructure projects include: (a) Reconstruction of Raleigh-Millington Road with improved drainage and sidewalks (Public Works); (b) Replacement of aging water mains serving the neighborhoods east of Stage Road (coordinated with MLGW); (c) Traffic signal upgrades at 8 intersections along Stage Road and Austin Peay Highway; (d) Stormwater improvements addressing persistent flooding near the Loosahatchie River floodplain.

title: Germantown Parkway Corridor Development Framework
text:
Corridor Overview. Germantown Parkway serves as the primary north-south commercial and transportation corridor connecting the eastern suburbs to the Memphis metropolitan core. This framework addresses the 8-mile segment from Walnut Grove Road north to Stage Road, encompassing major retail centers, office parks, and residential communities in the Germantown and East Memphis areas.

Retail Evolution. Traditional strip commercial centers along Germantown Parkway face increased vacancy as retail patterns shift toward e-commerce and experiential destinations. The framework recommends: (a) Rezoning underperforming retail parcels to allow mixed-use development with residential above commercial; (b) Consolidation of excess parking areas into development pads, reducing impervious surface and improving stormwater management; (c) Creation of lifestyle center nodes at key intersections — Walnut Grove Road, Poplar Avenue, and Dexter Road — with pedestrian-oriented design and structured parking; (d) Attraction of medical office, fitness, and dining uses to replace general merchandise retail.

Transportation Improvements. Germantown Parkway currently operates as a high-speed arterial with limited pedestrian access. The framework proposes: (a) Reduction of travel lanes from 6 to 4 on the segment between Walnut Grove Road and Poplar Avenue, with the reclaimed space dedicated to a center turn lane, bike lanes, and widened sidewalks; (b) Roundabouts at 3 intersections to improve traffic flow and reduce crashes; (c) MATA express bus service connecting the Germantown Parkway corridor to the Downtown transit hub; (d) Multi-use path along the east side of the parkway connecting Shelby Farms Park to the Wolf River Greenway.

Sustainability. The corridor framework incorporates green infrastructure: (a) Bioretention cells in all new parking lot developments; (b) Tree canopy requirements of 30% for commercial parcels and 40% for residential parcels; (c) Dark sky-compliant lighting standards for all new development to reduce light pollution affecting nearby Shelby Farms Park; (d) Electric vehicle charging stations required in developments with more than 100 parking spaces.

Implementation. The framework will be implemented over 10 years through a combination of public infrastructure investment ($25 million from the Capital Improvement Program), private development incentives (TIF district designation for the Poplar/Germantown Parkway node), and updated zoning regulations adopted by the Memphis and Shelby County Joint Planning Commission.

title: Memphis Medical District Innovation Hub Plan
text:
District Context. The Memphis Medical District, centered on Union Avenue between Danny Thomas Boulevard and Bellevue Boulevard, is the city's largest employment cluster with over 20,000 workers across Methodist Le Bonheur Healthcare, the University of Tennessee Health Science Center, St. Jude Children's Research Hospital, Regional One Health, and numerous medical offices. This plan transforms the medical campus area into a connected innovation district.

Innovation Ecosystem. The plan proposes a Memphis BioWorks Innovation Center on a 3-acre site at Union Avenue and Pauline Street: (a) 60,000-square-foot wet lab and co-working facility for biotech startups; (b) Technology transfer office bridging UTHSC research with commercial applications; (c) FDA regulatory guidance and clinical trial support services; (d) Conference center for medical innovation events. The facility would be developed through a public-private partnership with $20 million in state and city incentives and $30 million in private investment.

Housing for Workers. Medical district employees face housing challenges, with many commuting from distant suburbs. The plan addresses this through: (a) 500 new workforce housing units within walking distance of major employers; (b) Employer-assisted housing program offering down payment assistance to medical district workers purchasing homes in Midtown; (c) Micro-unit development targeting medical residents and students near UTHSC; (d) Conversion of obsolete office buildings on Madison Avenue into residential units.

Connectivity. The plan improves district connectivity through: (a) A dedicated shuttle circulator connecting all major medical campus buildings with 10-minute headways during business hours; (b) Protected bike lanes on Union Avenue and Pauline Street; (c) Pedestrian bridge over the CSX railroad tracks connecting the medical campus to the Edge District; (d) Wayfinding system with consistent signage across all medical institutions. Public Works will lead the streetscape and bike lane projects.

Public Health Integration. The innovation hub will include community-facing health resources: (a) Neighborhood clinic offering primary care and preventive services to surrounding communities; (b) Community health worker training program; (c) Public fitness trail connecting Forrest Park to the Memphis Greenline; (d) Mental health and substance abuse treatment center accessible to uninsured patients.

title: Shelby Farms Greenway Master Plan
text:
Vision. The Shelby Farms Greenway Master Plan envisions a continuous 20-mile network of multi-use trails connecting Shelby Farms Park to neighborhoods throughout east Memphis and beyond. Building on the success of the Shelby Farms Greenline — the region's most popular recreational trail — this plan extends green corridor access to underserved communities and creates the backbone of a regional trail network.

Trail Network. The plan identifies 4 priority trail segments: (a) Wolf River Trail Extension — 5.2 miles from Germantown Parkway west to Highland Street, following the Wolf River corridor through wooded floodplain; (b) Nonconnah Creek Greenway — 4.8 miles from Shelby Farms south to Hickory Hill, providing trail access to the Hickory Ridge area; (c) East Memphis Connector — 3.1 miles linking Shelby Farms Park to Oak Court Mall and the Walnut Grove Road corridor via neighborhood greenways; (d) Northern Greenline Extension — 6.5 miles from the current Greenline terminus at Tillman Street east through Binghampton and Normal Station to Shelby Farms.

Design Standards. All trail segments will follow these design standards managed by Parks & Recreation: (a) Minimum 12-foot paved surface width accommodating pedestrians, cyclists, and wheelchair users; (b) ADA-compliant grade with maximum 5% slope except where terrain requires switchbacks with rest areas; (c) Mile markers, emergency call stations, and wayfinding signage at all intersections; (d) Native plant landscaping in a minimum 10-foot buffer zone on each side of the trail; (e) Low-impact lighting at trailheads and road crossings using solar-powered LED fixtures.

Trailhead Facilities. The plan includes 8 new trailheads with: (a) Parking for 30-50 vehicles; (b) Restrooms and water fountains; (c) Bike repair stations; (d) Information kiosks with trail maps and safety guidelines; (e) Connection points to MATA transit stops where feasible. The Germantown Parkway trailhead will include a trail center with ranger office, meeting room, and outdoor education area.

Funding. The $48 million project will be funded through: (a) Federal Transportation Alternatives Program (TAP) grants — $15 million; (b) Tennessee Department of Transportation recreational trails fund — $5 million; (c) City of Memphis Capital Improvement Program — $10 million; (d) Private philanthropy including Shelby Farms Park Conservancy — $8 million; (e) Land and Water Conservation Fund — $5 million; (f) Corporate sponsorships and naming rights — $5 million. Construction is phased over 8 years with the Wolf River Trail Extension as the first priority segment.

title: Mud Island Redevelopment Concept Plan
text:
Site Analysis. Mud Island, a peninsula in the Mississippi River connected to Downtown Memphis by the Auction Avenue Bridge, encompasses approximately 500 acres of land including the Harbor Town planned community, the former Mud Island River Park, and significant undeveloped areas. The island's unique riverfront location, views of the downtown skyline, and proximity to the central business district make it one of Memphis's most valuable redevelopment opportunities.

Redevelopment Framework. The concept plan divides Mud Island into four development zones: (a) Harbor Town Expansion — infill of remaining residential lots in the existing Harbor Town community along Mud Island Road, adding 150 single-family homes and 200 multifamily units maintaining the neighborhood's new urbanist design principles; (b) River Park District — transformation of the former amusement park site into a mixed-use cultural destination with a river history museum, outdoor amphitheater, restaurants, and public gardens; (c) Marina Village — 25-acre waterfront development with 300 residential units, marina facilities, retail, and a boutique hotel; (d) Conservation Area — 100 acres of preserved wetland and riparian habitat along the island's north and west shores.

Infrastructure Requirements. Mud Island development requires significant infrastructure investment: (a) Replacement of the existing monorail with a modern vehicular and pedestrian bridge — estimated cost $35 million; (b) Flood protection improvements including raised building pads and an expanded levee system — $12 million; (c) Water and sewer infrastructure extension to undeveloped parcels — $8 million coordinated with MLGW; (d) Stormwater management system designed for the 100-year flood event; (e) Fire station satellite facility on the island to meet emergency response time standards (Fire Department).

Public Access. The plan ensures that Mud Island's riverfront remains publicly accessible: (a) Continuous public riverwalk trail around the island's perimeter; (b) Public boat launch and fishing pier on the west shore; (c) Free ferry service from the Downtown waterfront to the River Park District; (d) Public parks comprising at least 30% of the island's total area.

Financing. The plan proposes a Mud Island Special District with dedicated TIF revenue and assessment authority. Total public investment is estimated at $65 million over 15 years, leveraging $200 million in private development. Housing & Community Development and Planning & Development will jointly manage the implementation timeline.

title: Uptown Tax Increment Financing District Plan
text:
District Establishment. The Uptown Tax Increment Financing (TIF) District is established to support the redevelopment of the northern edge of Downtown Memphis, encompassing the area bounded by I-40 to the north, Danny Thomas Boulevard to the east, Chelsea Avenue to the south, and the Mississippi River to the west. This area includes the historic Pinch District along North Main Street and the former Hurt Village public housing site.

Redevelopment Goals. The Uptown TIF District aims to: (a) Create a mixed-income residential neighborhood of 1,500 housing units, including 30% affordable units at or below 60% AMI; (b) Develop 200,000 square feet of commercial and office space; (c) Establish a neighborhood retail center with a grocery store, pharmacy, and community services; (d) Connect the neighborhood to the Downtown core through improved streetscapes and transit. The district builds on the success of Uptown Square, a 400-unit mixed-income community completed in 2019.

TIF Mechanics. The base assessed property value for the district is $12.5 million (2024 assessment). All incremental property tax revenue above this base will be captured by the TIF district for a 30-year period. Projected incremental revenue totals $45 million over the life of the district. TIF proceeds will fund: (a) Public infrastructure — streets, sidewalks, water, and sewer (50% of revenue); (b) Affordable housing subsidies (25%); (c) Commercial development incentives (15%); (d) District administration and debt service (10%).

Phasing Plan. Phase 1 (2025-2027): Infrastructure improvements on North Main Street and Chelsea Avenue, including Complete Streets redesign with bike lanes, street trees, and decorative lighting. Groundbreaking for 300 residential units and 50,000 square feet of retail. Public Works and MLGW will coordinate utility upgrades. Phase 2 (2028-2030): Second residential phase of 500 units, office building development, and neighborhood park construction. Phase 3 (2031-2035): Final residential development, community center completion, and full build-out of commercial space.

Community Protections. To prevent displacement of existing North Memphis residents, the TIF plan includes: (a) Right of first refusal for displaced former public housing residents; (b) Anti-displacement fund providing relocation assistance and rental subsidies; (c) Community advisory board with at least 5 seats reserved for current neighborhood residents; (d) Local hiring requirement of 30% Memphis residents for all TIF-funded construction. Housing & Community Development will monitor compliance.

title: Memphis City Council Regular Meeting Minutes - January 7, 2025
text:
Call to Order. The Memphis City Council convened in regular session at 3:30 PM at Memphis City Hall, 125 North Main Street, Downtown Memphis. Council Chair presiding. Quorum present with 11 of 13 members in attendance.

Public Comment Period. Twelve citizens addressed the Council during the public comment period. James Wright of Midtown spoke regarding pothole conditions on Poplar Avenue near Overton Square, requesting expedited repair by Public Works. Patricia Coleman of Cooper-Young raised concerns about short-term rental noise violations on Young Avenue. Willie Jackson of North Memphis advocated for increased Solid Waste Management services to address illegal dumping on Chelsea Avenue. Sandra Harris of East Memphis requested a review of street light outage response times on Walnut Grove Road.

Consent Agenda. The following items were approved unanimously: (a) Minutes of the December 17, 2024 regular session; (b) Accounts payable vouchers totaling $14.2 million; (c) Renewal of the Solid Waste Management collection contract with Republic Services for FY2026 at $42.5 million; (d) Purchase of 12 replacement vehicles for Public Works fleet at $1.8 million.

Ordinance First Reading. Ordinance 2025-001: Amendment to Chapter 6.22 (Short-Term Rental Regulations) expanding permit requirements to all residential zones citywide. Currently limited to Cooper-Young and Midtown overlay districts. Referred to the Land Use and Government Committee for public hearing on January 21, 2025.

Resolutions. Resolution 2025-003: Authorizing the Mayor's Office to submit a $5 million RAISE grant application to the U.S. Department of Transportation for the Danny Thomas Boulevard Complete Streets project. Resolution adopted 11-0. Resolution 2025-004: Recognizing the Crosstown Community Development Corporation for achieving LEED Platinum certification at the Crosstown Concourse Arts Building. Resolution adopted 11-0.

Budget Update. The Finance Director presented the mid-year FY2026 budget update. General Fund revenue is tracking 3.2% above projections, primarily due to higher-than-expected sales tax collections. Property tax collections are at 94% of the annual levy. The 311 Call Center reported a 12% increase in call volume compared to the same period last year. Council directed the Finance Director to prepare options for supplemental appropriations to Public Works for pothole repair and to Parks & Recreation for the Overton Park trail improvement project.

Adjournment. Meeting adjourned at 5:45 PM. Next regular session scheduled for January 21, 2025.

title: Memphis City Council Regular Meeting Minutes - February 4, 2025
text:
Call to Order. The Memphis City Council convened in regular session at 3:30 PM at Memphis City Hall. Council Chair presiding. All 13 members present.

Public Comment Period. Eight citizens addressed the Council. Dorothy Lewis of Whitehaven praised the Elvis Presley Boulevard streetscape improvements and requested additional wayfinding signage near Graceland. Anthony Brown of Frayser reported recurring flooding issues on Stage Road near Raleigh-Millington Road and requested a Public Works assessment. Maria Garcia of Crosstown, speaking through an interpreter, asked about bilingual options for the Memphis 311 system. Michael Brooks of Downtown expressed concern about panhandling near FedExForum and requested Police Community Relations engagement.

Ordinance Second Reading and Adoption. Ordinance 2025-001 (Short-Term Rental citywide expansion): Following public hearing testimony from 23 citizens (15 in favor, 8 opposed), the ordinance was adopted 9-4. Key amendments added during committee: owner notification requirement for all adjacent properties within 200 feet, annual inspection requirement by Code Enforcement, and revenue reporting requirement to the Finance Director.

New Ordinance Introduction. Ordinance 2025-005: Establishing a Commercial Property Vacancy Registration program requiring owners of commercial properties vacant for more than 180 days to register with Planning & Development and pay an annual registration fee of $500. Fee revenue dedicated to blight remediation. Referred to the Economic Development Committee.

Capital Improvement Program. The Public Works Director presented the FY2026-2028 Capital Improvement Program update. Key projects include: (a) Danny Thomas Boulevard Complete Streets ($8.5 million, construction start Q3 2025); (b) Poplar Avenue resurfacing from Highland Street to Perkins Road ($3.2 million); (c) Frayser drainage improvements ($4.1 million, matching state funds); (d) Sidewalk replacement program citywide ($2 million annually). Council authorized supplemental appropriation of $1.5 million for emergency pothole repair from the General Fund reserve.

Appointments. Council confirmed the Mayor's appointment of a new Director of Parks & Recreation effective March 1, 2025. Council also confirmed appointments to the Memphis Historic Preservation Commission (2 new members) and the Board of Adjustment (1 vacancy).

Adjournment. Meeting adjourned at 6:15 PM. Next regular session scheduled for February 18, 2025.

title: Memphis City Council Regular Meeting Minutes - March 4, 2025
text:
Call to Order. The Memphis City Council convened in regular session at 3:30 PM at Memphis City Hall. Council Chair presiding. Twelve of 13 members present (1 excused absence).

Public Comment Period. Six citizens addressed the Council. Keisha Turner of Soulsville advocated for increased investment in the South Memphis Workforce Hub and reported a water main break on McLemore Avenue. Robert Chen of Germantown discussed traffic congestion on Germantown Parkway and supported the proposed road diet study. Two representatives from the Memphis Area Transit Authority (MATA) presented ridership data showing a 7% increase in bus ridership for FY2026 Q1 compared to the prior year.

Ordinance Adoption. Ordinance 2025-005 (Commercial Property Vacancy Registration): Adopted 10-2 after committee amendments reducing the initial registration threshold from 180 days to 365 days of vacancy and adding a waiver process for properties under active renovation with a valid building permit.

Budget Amendments. Council approved the following FY2026 budget amendments: (a) $2.3 million supplemental appropriation to Public Works for emergency storm damage repair following the February 15 severe weather event; (b) Transfer of $500,000 from the General Fund contingency to the Fire Department for overtime costs associated with the winter storm response; (c) $1.2 million grant acceptance from the Tennessee Department of Environment and Conservation for the Nonconnah Creek stormwater improvement project.

Reports. The 311 Call Center Director presented the quarterly performance report: 87,500 service requests received in Q1 FY2026 (12% increase year-over-year), average response time of 2.3 days (down from 2.8 days), top categories were Pothole (22%), Trash/Litter (18%), and Water/Sewer (15%). The Code Enforcement Director reported 450 blighted property citations issued and 120 demolitions completed in Q1.

Resolutions. Resolution 2025-012: Supporting the Memphis in May International Festival use of Tom Lee Park from April 25 through June 1, 2025. Adopted unanimously. Resolution 2025-013: Declaring the week of March 10-14 as Memphis Public Works Appreciation Week. Adopted unanimously.

Executive Session. Council entered executive session at 5:30 PM to discuss pending litigation. No action taken. Meeting adjourned at 6:00 PM.

title: Memphis City Council Regular Meeting Minutes - April 1, 2025
text:
Call to Order. The Memphis City Council convened in regular session at 3:30 PM at Memphis City Hall. Council Chair presiding. All 13 members present.

Public Comment Period. Fourteen citizens addressed the Council, the highest attendance of the fiscal year. Topics included: (a) Residents from Orange Mound presented a petition with 340 signatures supporting the Orange Mound Historic District National Register nomination; (b) Business owners from Broad Avenue in Binghampton requested extension of the Arts District tax incentives beyond the current 2026 expiration; (c) Homeowners from Hickory Hill reported persistent code enforcement delays for abandoned properties near Hickory Ridge Mall; (d) The Memphis Greenways Coalition advocated for accelerated funding of the Shelby Farms Greenway Northern Extension.

FY2027 Budget Preview. The Mayor presented the preliminary FY2027 budget framework: (a) Total General Fund budget of $715 million (3.1% increase over FY2026); (b) Property tax rate maintained at the current $3.19 per $100 assessed value; (c) New investment priorities: $5 million increase for Public Works street resurfacing, $3 million for Parks & Recreation facility improvements including Overton Park and the Orange Mound Community Center, $2 million for Code Enforcement additional inspectors (8 new positions), and $1.5 million for MATA service expansion. Council set the public hearing on the FY2027 budget for May 6, 2025.

Ordinance Introduction. Ordinance 2025-010: Establishing a Memphis Community Land Trust to acquire, hold, and steward permanently affordable housing in gentrifying neighborhoods. Initial focus areas: Binghampton, Crosstown, and the Medical District. Administered by Housing & Community Development with a $3 million initial capitalization from the Housing Trust Fund. Referred to the Housing Committee.

Contracts. Council approved: (a) Professional services contract with AECOM for the Danny Thomas Boulevard Complete Streets design — $1.2 million; (b) Construction contract for Frayser drainage improvements Phase 1 — $3.8 million; (c) Technology services agreement for the new Memphis 311 mobile application — $450,000 over 3 years.

Adjournment. Meeting adjourned at 6:30 PM. Next regular session scheduled for April 15, 2025.

title: City of Memphis Annual Budget Summary FY2026
text:
Executive Summary. The City of Memphis FY2026 annual budget totals $2.1 billion across all funds, with a General Fund budget of $693 million. The budget maintains essential city services, funds strategic investments in infrastructure and public safety, and holds the property tax rate at $3.19 per $100 of assessed value for the third consecutive year.

General Fund Revenue. Projected General Fund revenue of $693 million includes: Property taxes — $268 million (38.7%); Local option sales tax — $172 million (24.8%); State shared revenue — $89 million (12.8%); Licenses and permits — $34 million (4.9%); Charges for services — $52 million (7.5%); Intergovernmental grants — $43 million (6.2%); Other revenue including investment income and fines — $35 million (5.1%).

Departmental Allocations. Police Department: $218 million (31.5% of General Fund) — includes 2,100 sworn officers and 600 civilian staff, $8 million for equipment replacement, and $3 million for the Real Time Crime Center technology upgrade. Fire Department: $128 million (18.5%) — includes 1,400 firefighters across 50 stations, $5 million for apparatus replacement. Public Works: $72 million (10.4%) — includes $22 million for street resurfacing, $8 million for sidewalk and drainage maintenance, and $12 million for traffic signal and street lighting operations. Parks & Recreation: $38 million (5.5%) — includes $6 million for facility maintenance across 160 parks, $4 million for programming, and $3.2 million for the Overton Park improvement project.

Capital Improvement Program. The FY2026-2030 CIP totals $285 million: Public Works infrastructure — $120 million (street resurfacing, bridges, drainage); Public safety facilities — $45 million (fire station renovations, police precinct upgrades); Parks and community facilities — $38 million (Shelby Farms Greenway, community center renovations); Technology — $22 million (311 system upgrade, permitting software, cybersecurity); General government — $60 million (fleet replacement, building maintenance, ADA compliance).

Debt Management. The city's outstanding general obligation debt is $890 million with an average interest rate of 3.8%. The debt-to-assessed-value ratio is 4.2%, well below the 10% ceiling established by city charter. Annual debt service payments total $105 million (15.2% of General Fund revenue), within the city's target of 20% maximum.

title: Memphis Parks & Recreation FY2026 Program Schedule
text:
Department Overview. Memphis Parks & Recreation manages 160 parks, 12 community centers, 7 golf courses, 4 public swimming pools, and over 100 miles of trails serving the Memphis community. The FY2026 program schedule reflects expanded programming at community centers, new trail initiatives, and enhanced seasonal event offerings.

Community Center Programs.
